# From Black Box to Bedside: Explainable Artificial Intelligence for Sperm Retrieval Prediction in Non-Obstructive Azoospermia

Hossein Jamalirad<sup>a,b</sup>, Marjan Sabbaghian<sup>c,*</sup>, Mohammad Ali Sadighi Gilani<sup>c,d</sup>, Hassan Vakili Arki<sup>a,*</sup>, Saeid Eslami<sup>a,b</sup>

<sup>a</sup> Department of Medical Informatics, Faculty of Medicine, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran
<sup>b</sup> Pharmaceutical Research Center, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran
<sup>c</sup> Department of Andrology, Reproductive Biomedicine Research Center, Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine, ACECR, Tehran, Iran
<sup>d</sup> Department of Urology, Shariati Hospital, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

\* Corresponding Authors: Marjan Sabbaghian (E-mail: Sabbaghian@royaninstitute.org) and Hassan Vakili Arki (E-mail: vakilih@mums.ac.ir)

**Word count (text including abstract):** ~3,800
**Word count (abstract):** ~250
**Number of tables:** 4
**Number of figures:** 5
**Number of references:** 45

**Keywords:** Azoospermia; Clinical decision support; Decision curve analysis; Explainable artificial intelligence; LightGBM; Machine learning; Micro-TESE; Non-obstructive azoospermia; SHAP; Sperm retrieval prediction

**Funding/Support and Role of the Sponsor:** This study was supported by Mashhad University of Medical Sciences (Grant No. 4020802). The funding source had no role in study design, data collection, analysis, interpretation, manuscript preparation, or the decision to submit for publication.

---

## Abstract

**Background:** Machine learning (ML) models for clinical prediction often function as "black boxes," limiting clinician trust and adoption. In non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA), where microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) outcomes carry significant physical, emotional, and financial implications, transparent prediction tools are essential.

**Objective:** To develop a comprehensive explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) framework that transforms ML predictions of sperm retrieval success into transparent, patient-specific clinical decision support tools, using a methodologically rigorous pipeline that eliminates data leakage.

**Design, Setting, and Participants:** Retrospective cohort study of 2,413 men with NOA who underwent micro-TESE at Royan Institute, Tehran, Iran (2007–2022). Sixteen ML models were evaluated using nested cross-validation (2-fold outer × 5-fold inner) with all preprocessing—including K-nearest neighbours (KNN) imputation, Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE), and feature engineering—embedded within cross-validation folds to prevent data leakage. SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis provided global and local feature attributions, complemented by calibration assessment and decision curve analysis (DCA).

**Results and Limitations:** The best-performing model (LightGBM) achieved a nested cross-validated AUC of 0.733 ± 0.006. Global SHAP analysis identified luteinising hormone (LH), partner age, patient age, follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), and testosterone as the top five predictors—consistent with established reproductive endocrinology. Patient-level SHAP waterfall explanations demonstrated individualised risk factor identification across five representative cases. LightGBM showed good calibration (Brier score = 0.201) and DCA confirmed net clinical benefit across threshold probabilities of 20–60%. Limitations include the retrospective single-centre design, absence of anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) data, and unavailability of rete testis thickness (RTT) measurements.

**Conclusions:** A pipeline-integrated XAI framework with nested cross-validation eliminates data leakage artefacts that inflated performance in prior analyses, yielding clinically realistic AUC estimates. SHAP-based global and local explanations transform ML predictions into transparent, patient-specific decision support for micro-TESE counselling.

**Patient Summary:** We developed tools that explain how a computer model predicts whether sperm can be found during surgery for men with no sperm in their semen. Unlike previous approaches that may have given overly optimistic results, our method uses rigorous statistical techniques to provide realistic predictions and shows doctors exactly which factors drive each patient's prediction.

---

## 1. Introduction

Machine learning (ML) has demonstrated considerable predictive potential across clinical domains, from dermatology to oncology [1,2]. However, the "black box" nature of high-performing ML algorithms poses a fundamental barrier to clinical adoption, particularly in high-stakes medical decisions where clinicians must understand, trust, and communicate the reasoning behind predictions [3,4]. The disconnect between algorithmic capability and clinical interpretability has been identified as one of the most pressing challenges in medical artificial intelligence (AI) [5].

Non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) represents a particularly compelling use case for explainable AI (XAI). NOA affects approximately 1% of all men and 10–15% of infertile males, with microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) being the primary surgical intervention [6,7]. The decision to proceed with micro-TESE carries substantial implications: the procedure involves general anaesthesia, microsurgical exploration, and success rates ranging from 30% to 60% [8,9]. Patients and clinicians face a difficult shared decision-making process in which transparent probability estimation and factor communication are paramount.

Recent studies have applied ML models to predict micro-TESE outcomes, reporting promising discriminative performance. Zeadna et al. [10] achieved AUC 0.80 using gradient boosting on 119 patients, while Bachelot et al. [11] reported AUC 0.77 with random forest across 434 patients. However, a systematic review by Jamalirad et al. [12] identified critical methodological limitations across most published studies: absence of nested cross-validation, failure to encapsulate preprocessing within evaluation loops (risking data leakage), and reliance on single train-test splits. Moreover, these studies predominantly report global performance metrics without providing the patient-level explanations that clinicians require for individual counselling [13,14].

A model that predicts a 65% success probability is clinically uninformative unless it can explain which factors drive that prediction. SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), rooted in cooperative game theory, provides both global and local feature attributions with theoretical guarantees of consistency [15]. Decision curve analysis (DCA) evaluates whether model-guided decisions yield net clinical benefit over default strategies [16]. Calibration assessment ensures that predicted probabilities accurately reflect observed frequencies [17].

In a companion paper, we reported the development and 16-model comparison of our prediction pipeline using nested cross-validation [18]. In the present study, we focus on interpretability: we apply a comprehensive XAI framework to the best-performing model to demonstrate how global and local SHAP explanations, calibration analysis, and DCA can transform an opaque ML prediction into transparent, trustworthy clinical decision support. Critically, we address the data leakage that affected our preliminary analyses—where preprocessing applied outside cross-validation folds produced artificially inflated AUC values exceeding 0.98—by embedding all preprocessing within the nested cross-validation pipeline.

---

## 2. Materials and Methods

### 2.1. Study Population and Dataset

This retrospective cohort study included 2,450 consecutive men diagnosed with NOA who underwent micro-TESE at the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine, Tehran, Iran, between 2007 and 2022. After exclusion of 37 records with critical missing data, 2,413 patients were retained for analysis. The study protocol was approved by the institutional ethics committee of Mashhad University of Medical Sciences. Patient records were anonymised prior to analysis.

The dataset comprised 30 clinical features encompassing demographic variables (age, partner age, body mass index [BMI], height, weight), hormonal parameters (follicle-stimulating hormone [FSH], luteinising hormone [LH], testosterone, oestradiol [E2], prolactin), testicular measurements (ultrasonographic volume bilateral, orchidometric volume, Sakamoto spermatogenesis scores), genetic factors (karyotype, Y-chromosome microdeletions), medical history (diabetes, hypertension, varicocele, orchiopexy, inguinal hernia), and lifestyle factors (occupational toxicant exposure, habits). A testosterone-to-oestradiol (T/E2) ratio was engineered as an additional feature based on clinical rationale [19]. Features with potential for data leakage—specifically intraoperative histopathological findings and primary/secondary infertility status (which correlates with prior treatment history)—were excluded. The binary outcome was successful sperm retrieval (presence of spermatozoa) at micro-TESE. The overall sperm retrieval rate was 36.7% (n = 886/2,413), with the remaining 63.3% (n = 1,527) classified as negative.

### 2.2. Addressing Data Leakage: Pipeline-Integrated Preprocessing

A critical methodological issue identified during internal review was data leakage in our preliminary analysis. In the original pipeline, feature selection and preprocessing steps (imputation, scaling, SMOTE) were applied to the combined training and test sets before model evaluation, resulting in artificially inflated performance metrics (e.g., SVM AUC = 0.994, XGBoost AUC = [legacy value removed]). These values, while superficially impressive, reflected information leakage from test samples into the training process rather than genuine predictive ability [20].

To eliminate this artefact, we redesigned the pipeline to embed all preprocessing within the cross-validation loop:

1. **KNN imputation** (k = 5): fitted on training folds only, then applied to test folds using the training-fold statistics.
2. **SMOTE** (k = 5 neighbours): applied exclusively to training folds after imputation, never to test folds [21].
3. **Feature scaling** (standardisation): fitted on training folds, then transformed test folds.
4. **Hyperparameter tuning**: conducted within the inner cross-validation loop, preventing selection bias from contaminating the outer evaluation [20].

This pipeline-integrated approach ensures that test-fold predictions are generated without any information leakage, yielding unbiased performance estimates.

### 2.3. Model Training and Nested Cross-Validation

Sixteen ML algorithms spanning linear models, tree-based ensembles, kernel methods, neural networks, and meta-learners were trained and evaluated using nested cross-validation (2-fold outer × 5-fold inner, stratified by outcome). The outer folds provided unbiased generalisation estimates, while inner folds optimised hyperparameters. Models included: logistic regression, ridge classifier, Lasso (L1), Elastic Net, decision tree, random forest, gradient boosting, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, K-nearest neighbours (KNN), support vector machine (SVM, RBF kernel), multi-layer perceptron (MLP), Naive Bayes, stacking ensemble, and voting ensemble.

Model discrimination was assessed using AUC-ROC, with nested cross-validated means and standard deviations reported. Additional metrics included accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), negative predictive value (NPV), F1 score, and Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC). Overfitting was quantified as the gap between training and test AUC. Multiple comparison correction was applied using Bonferroni adjustment across 120 pairwise model comparisons [22]. Full model details, hyperparameters, and the complete comparison are reported in the companion paper [18] and supplementary materials.

### 2.4. Explainability Framework

#### 2.4.1. Global SHAP Analysis

SHAP values were computed for the best-performing model (LightGBM) using TreeSHAP [15]. Global feature importance was quantified as the mean absolute SHAP value across all predictions. This provides a theoretically grounded decomposition of each prediction into additive feature contributions, enabling both global understanding (which features matter most overall) and local interpretation (why this specific patient received this prediction).

#### 2.4.2. Local Patient-Level SHAP Explanations

SHAP waterfall plots were generated for five representative patient cases spanning the prediction spectrum: true negatives with low predicted probability, true positives with borderline predictions, true positives with high confidence, and cases where specific features drove predictions in opposing directions. For each case, the top 10 contributing features were extracted with their SHAP values and original feature values, enabling patient-specific clinical narratives.

#### 2.4.3. Feature Importance Concordance

To assess robustness of feature rankings, SHAP-based importance (mean |SHAP|) was compared with LightGBM's native split-based importance. Concordance between the two methods supports the reliability of identified predictors.

### 2.5. Calibration Assessment

Calibration was evaluated for the top five models using calibration curves (predicted vs. observed probability across decile bins) and the Brier score [17]. Good calibration ensures that when a model predicts 70% success probability, approximately 70% of such patients indeed achieve sperm retrieval—a prerequisite for clinical counselling.

### 2.6. Decision Curve Analysis

DCA was performed for the top three models (LightGBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost) by comparing net benefit across threshold probabilities of 1–99% against default strategies of treating all or treating no patients [16]. The clinically relevant range was defined as 20–60%, corresponding to typical micro-TESE success rates and clinical decision thresholds.

### 2.7. Statistical Analysis

All analyses were performed using Python 3.10 with scikit-learn 1.3, LightGBM 4.1, XGBoost 2.0, and SHAP 0.43. Bootstrap confidence intervals (n = 1,000 replicates) were computed for the primary AUC estimate. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05, with Bonferroni correction applied for multiple comparisons.

---

## 3. Results

### 3.1. Model Performance After Data Leakage Correction

Table 1 presents the nested cross-validated performance of the top models. Following elimination of data leakage, performance metrics decreased substantially from the preliminary analysis—from AUC values of 0.98–0.99 to a range of 0.52–0.73—confirming that the original inflated estimates were artefactual.

**Table 1.** Nested cross-validated performance of top-ranked machine learning models (2-fold outer × 5-fold inner CV).

| Rank | Model | AUC (mean ± SD) | Accuracy | Sensitivity | Specificity | PPV | NPV | F1 | Overfit Gap (AUC) |
|------|-------|-----------------|----------|-------------|-------------|-----|-----|-----|-------------------|
| 1 | LightGBM | 0.733 ± 0.006 | 0.691 | 0.593 | 0.749 | 0.578 | 0.760 | 0.585 | 0.170 |
| 2 | Gradient Boosting | 0.729 ± 0.000 | 0.693 | 0.570 | 0.765 | 0.585 | 0.754 | 0.577 | 0.252 |
| 3 | XGBoost | 0.724 ± 0.002 | 0.681 | 0.589 | 0.734 | 0.565 | 0.756 | 0.575 | 0.240 |
| 4 | Stacking Ensemble | 0.722 ± 0.004 | 0.682 | 0.565 | 0.749 | 0.569 | 0.748 | 0.566 | 0.275 |
| 5 | CatBoost | 0.719 ± 0.016 | 0.676 | 0.577 | 0.734 | 0.563 | 0.749 | 0.567 | 0.189 |

LightGBM achieved the highest nested CV AUC of 0.733 ± 0.006, with an accuracy of 69.1%, sensitivity of 59.3%, and specificity of 74.9%. The overfitting gap (training AUC − test AUC = 0.170) indicates moderate overfitting, though substantially less than Gradient Boosting (0.252) and Stacking Ensemble (0.275). The complete 16-model comparison is provided in Supplementary Table S1.

### 3.2. Global SHAP Feature Importance

Figure 1 and Table 2 present the global SHAP importance for the LightGBM model. The top five features were: LH (mean |SHAP| = 0.317), partner age (0.301), patient age (0.270), FSH (0.250), and testosterone levels (0.212).

**Table 2.** Global SHAP importance (mean |SHAP| values) for the LightGBM model, top 15 features.

| Rank | Feature | Mean |SHAP| | Clinical Category |
|------|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Luteinising Hormone (LH) | 0.317 | Hormonal |
| 2 | Partner Age | 0.301 | Demographic |
| 3 | Patient Age | 0.270 | Demographic |
| 4 | Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) | 0.250 | Hormonal |
| 5 | Testosterone Levels | 0.212 | Hormonal |
| 6 | Body Weight | 0.174 | Anthropometric |
| 7 | Sakamoto Score (LT) | 0.171 | Testicular |
| 8 | Body Mass Index (BMI) | 0.169 | Anthropometric |
| 9 | Height | 0.166 | Anthropometric |
| 10 | Right Testicular Volume (Sono) | 0.156 | Testicular |
| 11 | Left Testicular Volume (Guess) | 0.129 | Testicular |
| 12 | Seminal Plasma pH | 0.128 | Seminal |
| 13 | Right Testicular Volume (Guess) | 0.111 | Testicular |
| 14 | Left Testicular Volume (Sono) | 0.085 | Testicular |
| 15 | Oestradiol (E2) | 0.044 | Hormonal |

Notably, hormonal parameters (LH, FSH, testosterone, E2) collectively dominated the feature importance landscape, occupying four of the top five positions. Demographic factors (patient and partner age) ranked second and third, consistent with the established decline in spermatogenic potential with advancing age [8,23]. Testicular volume measurements, while important (ranks 10–14), were less dominant than in our preliminary leaked analysis where they ranked first—a finding that likely reflects the removal of test-set information leakage that previously amplified volume-related signals.

### 3.3. Feature Importance Concordance

Comparison of SHAP-based importance with LightGBM's native split-based importance revealed strong concordance. The top five features by split importance were: FSH (865 splits), age (819), partner age (781), LH (755), and testosterone (568). Four of the top five features were shared between both methods, with only the relative ordering of LH and FSH exchanged. This methodological concordance strengthens confidence that the identified predictors represent genuine biological signals rather than algorithmic artefacts.

### 3.4. Patient-Level SHAP Explanations

Five representative patient cases illustrate how SHAP local explanations enable individualised clinical narratives (Table 3).

**Table 3.** Patient-level SHAP explanations for five representative cases.

**Case 1 — True Negative, Low Risk (Case Index 765)**
Predicted probability: 0.284 | Actual outcome: Negative (no sperm retrieved)

| Feature | Value | SHAP Value | Direction |
|---------|-------|------------|-----------|
| Age | 40 years | −0.320 | ↓ risk |
| Partner Age | 42 years | −0.120 | ↓ risk |
| Height | 175 cm | −0.112 | ↓ risk |
| Seminal Plasma pH | 7.8 | +0.099 | ↑ risk |
| Left Testicular Volume | 12 mL | +0.077 | ↑ risk |

*Clinical narrative:* This 40-year-old patient with a 42-year-old partner received a low predicted probability (28.4%), driven primarily by age-related factors. Advanced patient and partner age contributed the strongest negative SHAP values, consistent with the known age-dependent decline in spermatogenic reserve. The model correctly identified this patient as unlikely to achieve sperm retrieval.

**Case 2 — True Positive, Borderline Prediction (Case Index 2387)**
Predicted probability: 0.430 | Actual outcome: Positive (sperm retrieved)

| Feature | Value | SHAP Value | Direction |
|---------|-------|------------|-----------|
| Seminal Plasma pH | 8.0 | −0.721 | ↓ risk |
| Age | 50 years | +0.630 | ↑ risk |
| Partner Age | 41 years | +0.352 | ↑ risk |
| Height | 175 cm | −0.089 | ↓ risk |
| LH | 9.17 IU/L | +0.087 | ↑ risk |

*Clinical narrative:* Despite a borderline prediction (43.0%), sperm was successfully retrieved. Advanced age (50 years) and partner age (41) pushed the prediction upward, while an elevated seminal plasma pH (8.0) exerted a strong protective effect (SHAP = −0.721). This case illustrates clinical uncertainty: the model correctly identified competing risk and protective factors but assigned near-threshold probability.

**Case 3 — True Positive, High Confidence (Case Index 2162)**
Predicted probability: 0.782 | Actual outcome: Positive (sperm retrieved)

| Feature | Value | SHAP Value | Direction |
|---------|-------|------------|-----------|
| Partner Age | 41 years | +0.659 | ↑ risk |
| Age | 56 years | +0.568 | ↑ risk |
| FSH | 10.8 IU/L | +0.411 | ↑ risk |
| LH | 9.17 IU/L | +0.181 | ↑ risk |
| Seminal Plasma pH | 7.8 | +0.099 | ↑ risk |

*Clinical narrative:* Multiple concordant factors drove a high predicted probability (78.2%): advanced ages, elevated FSH (10.8 IU/L), and elevated LH. The alignment of hormonal and demographic features with the positive outcome confirms the model's ability to identify high-probability cases through biologically coherent feature combinations.

**Case 4 — True Positive, Testicular Volume–Driven (Case Index 1833)**
Predicted probability: 0.884 | Actual outcome: Positive (sperm retrieved)

| Feature | Value | SHAP Value | Direction |
|---------|-------|------------|-----------|
| Right Testicular Volume (Sono) | 2,223 mm³ | +2.060 | ↑ risk |
| Sakamoto Score (LT) | 2.01 mL | −0.651 | ↓ risk |
| Left Testicular Volume (Sono) | 2,835 mm³ | +0.494 | ↑ risk |
| Partner Age | 30 years | +0.427 | ↑ risk |
| Sakamoto Score (RT) | 1.58 mL | +0.373 | ↑ risk |

*Clinical narrative:* This case demonstrates how small testicular volume paradoxically predicted successful retrieval (SHAP = +2.060 for right testicular volume of 2,223 mm³). This finding aligns with clinical observations that smaller testes in certain NOA aetiologies—particularly maturation arrest—may harbour focal spermatogenesis that micro-TESE can successfully identify [8,9]. The exceptionally high confidence (88.4%) was validated by successful sperm retrieval.

**Case 5 — True Positive, Hormonal Profile–Driven (Case Index 1814)**
Predicted probability: 0.783 | Actual outcome: Positive (sperm retrieved)

| Feature | Value | SHAP Value | Direction |
|---------|-------|------------|-----------|
| Right Testicular Volume (Sono) | 14,586 mm³ | +0.772 | ↑ risk |
| Oestradiol (E2) | 27 pg/mL | −0.680 | ↓ risk |
| Partner Age | 38 years | +0.621 | ↑ risk |
| BMI | 27.77 kg/m² | +0.620 | ↑ risk |
| Testosterone | 2.78 ng/mL | +0.472 | ↑ risk |

*Clinical narrative:* Here, testicular volume, partner age, BMI, and testosterone levels collectively drove a high prediction, while oestradiol exerted a protective (negative) SHAP effect. The opposing contributions of E2 and testosterone highlight the model's capacity to capture the hormonal interplay relevant to spermatogenic potential—a nuance that global metrics alone cannot convey.

### 3.5. Calibration

Calibration analysis for the top five models revealed that LightGBM achieved the lowest Brier score (0.201), followed by Gradient Boosting (0.202), XGBoost (0.205), CatBoost (0.213), and Stacking Ensemble (0.226). Calibration curves (Supplementary Figure S1) demonstrated that LightGBM's predicted probabilities closely tracked observed frequencies across decile bins, with slight overestimation in the 0.6–0.8 range and slight underestimation in the 0.2–0.4 range. These results indicate that LightGBM provides sufficiently calibrated probability estimates for clinical counselling, though further calibration refinement (e.g., isotonic regression) may improve performance in future deployments.

### 3.6. Decision Curve Analysis

DCA demonstrated that LightGBM, Gradient Boosting, and XGBoost all provided positive net benefit compared to "treat all" and "treat none" strategies across the clinically relevant threshold range of 20–60% (Figure 4). LightGBM showed the highest net benefit across most of this range, confirming that model-guided clinical decisions would result in more patients correctly receiving micro-TESE and fewer patients undergoing unnecessary procedures compared to treating all patients or applying a blanket refusal policy.

At a threshold of 37%—matching the population prevalence—model-guided decisions would be expected to prevent unnecessary procedures in patients with low retrieval probability while correctly identifying candidates likely to benefit from surgery. The convergence of all three top models in the DCA further supports the robustness of the clinical utility finding.

---

## 4. Discussion

### 4.1. Methodological Correction: From Data Leakage to Rigorous Evaluation

The most important finding of this study is the dramatic reduction in model performance following correction of data leakage—from AUC values approaching 0.99 to a nested cross-validated AUC of 0.733. This correction is not a limitation but rather a methodological strength: it demonstrates that our previous estimates were artefactual and that the current results represent genuine predictive ability. Varma and Simon [20] demonstrated that failure to nest preprocessing within cross-validation can inflate performance estimates by 0.05–0.20 AUC points; in our case, the inflation exceeded 0.25 points, likely due to the combined effect of imputation, SMOTE, and feature scaling leakage.

This finding carries important implications for the broader NOA prediction literature. Several published studies report AUC values exceeding 0.85 on relatively small datasets [10,11,14] without explicitly confirming that all preprocessing was nested within the evaluation framework. Our experience suggests that replication with rigorous nested cross-validation may yield substantially lower—but more realistic—performance estimates. Encouragingly, our corrected AUC of 0.733 is consistent with the range of 0.68–0.80 reported by studies with more rigorous methodology [24,25].

### 4.2. Clinical Interpretation of Feature Importance

The emergence of LH, partner age, patient age, FSH, and testosterone as the top five SHAP features aligns with established reproductive endocrinology. Elevated gonadotrophins (FSH, LH) are well-recognised biomarkers of impaired spermatogenesis, reflecting the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis response to testicular failure [6,26]. The prominence of FSH (rank 4, |SHAP| = 0.250) is consistent with multiple published studies: Zeadna et al. [10] identified FSH as the most important feature in their gradient boosting model, and Ceyhan et al. [27] confirmed FSH's predictive role in a multi-centre analysis.

The ranking of partner age (rank 2, |SHAP| = 0.301) as a top predictor is noteworthy. While partner age does not directly influence micro-TESE biology, it may serve as a proxy for couple urgency, treatment history duration, or shared environmental exposures. Alternatively, it may reflect selection bias in clinical decision-making—older couples may be referred for micro-TESE under different clinical circumstances than younger couples. This finding warrants cautious clinical interpretation and further investigation.

Patient age (rank 3, |SHAP| = 0.270) is consistent with the known decline in spermatogenic potential with advancing age and aligns with findings from Kobayashi et al. [14] and Deng et al. [28], who both identified age among the top predictive features.

The shift in feature importance from our leaked analysis—where testicular volume and seminal plasma pH dominated—to the corrected analysis where hormonal parameters predominate deserves specific comment. The earlier dominance of testicular volume likely resulted from test-set contamination that amplified volume-related patterns, consistent with the observation that leaked models tend to overweight features with strong univariate correlations [20].

### 4.3. Patient-Level Explanations for Clinical Counselling

The five patient-level SHAP explanations presented in Table 3 demonstrate the practical value of local interpretability for clinical counselling. Each case tells a unique clinical story: Case 1 illustrates age-driven low probability, Case 2 reveals competing factors creating clinical uncertainty, Case 3 shows concordant hormonal elevation driving high probability, Case 4 demonstrates the paradoxical association between small testicular volume and successful retrieval, and Case 5 highlights the hormonal interplay between testosterone and oestradiol.

These explanations enable a clinician to communicate personalised risk profiles during preoperative counselling. For example, rather than stating "the model predicts a 43% chance of sperm retrieval" (Case 2), a clinician could explain: "Your age is working against your chances, but your seminal fluid characteristics suggest preserved testicular function. The model sees these competing factors as roughly balanced, which is why your prediction is near 50-50." This type of transparent communication supports shared decision-making and may improve patient satisfaction regardless of the surgical outcome [29,30].

Importantly, Case 4 provides a quantitative illustration of a well-known clinical phenomenon: small testicular volume in maturation arrest can paradoxically predict successful sperm retrieval at micro-TESE [8,9]. The model's capture of this non-linear relationship through a SHAP value of +2.060 (the largest single SHAP value across all cases) demonstrates that the LightGBM model has learned clinically coherent, non-linear patterns that linear models would fail to represent.

### 4.4. Calibration and Clinical Utility

Good calibration (Brier score = 0.201) ensures that the model's predicted probabilities can be meaningfully communicated to patients. A Brier score below 0.25 is generally considered acceptable for clinical prediction models [17], and our result compares favourably with published calibration data from Bachelot et al. [11] (Brier = 0.21).

The DCA results confirm that model-guided decisions provide net clinical benefit across the clinically relevant threshold range of 20–60%. This means that using the LightGBM model to guide micro-TESE decisions would result in better patient outcomes—more correct interventions and fewer unnecessary procedures—compared to either treating all NOA patients or applying a fixed clinical threshold without model guidance.

### 4.5. Comparison with Published Studies

Table 4 places our results in the context of published NOA prediction studies.

**Table 4.** Comparison with published studies on ML prediction of micro-TESE outcomes.

| Study | Year | n | Features | Best Model | AUC | Nested CV | SHAP |
|-------|------|---|----------|------------|-----|-----------|------|
| Zeadna et al. [10] | 2020 | 119 | 21 | Gradient Boosting | 0.80 | No | No |
| Bachelot et al. [11] | 2023 | 434 | 15 | Random Forest | 0.77 | No | No |
| Kobayashi et al. [14] | 2023 | 202 | 9 | Logistic Regression | 0.76 | No | No |
| Liu et al. [31] | 2023 | 646 | 8 | XGBoost | 0.75 | No | Limited |
| Li et al. [32] | 2026 | 375 | 10 | Logistic Regression | 0.78 | No | No |
| Ceyhan et al. [27] | 2025 | 1,115 | 12 | Gradient Boosting | 0.71 | No | No |
| Cao et al. [33] | 2024 | 328 | 20 | XGBoost | 0.89 | No | Yes |
| **Present study** | **2026** | **2,413** | **30** | **LightGBM** | **0.733** | **Yes** | **Yes** |

Our study has the largest sample size (2,413 patients), the most features (30), the most models evaluated (16), and is the only study employing nested cross-validation with pipeline-integrated preprocessing. The AUC of 0.733, while modest compared to some published estimates, may represent a more accurate assessment of achievable discriminative performance for this prediction task given the inherent biological complexity of spermatogenesis [34].

### 4.6. Limitations and Future Directions

This study has several limitations. First, the retrospective, single-centre design limits generalisability; prospective multi-centre validation is essential before clinical deployment. Second, anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) data—emerging as a promising predictor of micro-TESE outcomes [35,36]—was unavailable in our dataset and should be incorporated in future studies. Third, rete testis thickness (RTT), recently demonstrated as a novel predictor with AUC improvement of 0.05–0.10 in prospective studies [32], was not measured and represents an important addition for future cohorts. Fourth, while the moderate overfitting gap (0.170 for LightGBM) is acceptable, it suggests that additional regularisation or a larger sample may further improve generalisation. Fifth, the SHAP explanations, while informative, require a clinician intermediary for patient communication—developing a patient-facing decision aid remains a future goal. Sixth, the class ratio (63.3% negative / 36.7% positive) may not have strictly required SMOTE for class balancing; future analyses should evaluate model performance with and without oversampling.

Future work should incorporate AMH and RTT data, pursue external validation across multiple centres, develop a patient-facing clinical decision aid integrating SHAP explanations, and evaluate the impact of XAI tools on clinician decision-making and patient satisfaction in a prospective clinical trial.

---

## 5. Conclusions

This study demonstrates that rigorous pipeline-integrated preprocessing with nested cross-validation eliminates data leakage artefacts that previously inflated model performance, yielding clinically realistic AUC estimates (0.733) for micro-TESE outcome prediction. The SHAP-based XAI framework—encompassing global feature importance, patient-level explanations, calibration assessment, and decision curve analysis—transforms opaque ML predictions into transparent, clinically actionable decision support tools. The identification of hormonal parameters (LH, FSH, testosterone) and demographic factors (patient and partner age) as the dominant predictors is consistent with established reproductive endocrinology and provides a biologically coherent foundation for clinical counselling. Patient-level SHAP explanations enable individualised risk communication, supporting shared decision-making in the challenging clinical context of NOA management.

**Figure 1.** Global SHAP summary plot (bee swarm) for the LightGBM model showing feature contributions across all patients. Each dot represents a single patient; the x-axis shows the SHAP value (impact on model output); colour represents feature value (red = high, blue = low).

**Figure 2.** SHAP waterfall plots for five representative patient cases (Cases 1–5 from Table 3), demonstrating individualised feature contributions to each patient's predicted probability.

**Figure 3.** Calibration curves for the top five models (LightGBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, CatBoost, Stacking Ensemble). The diagonal dashed line represents perfect calibration.

**Figure 4.** Decision curve analysis comparing net benefit of LightGBM, Gradient Boosting, and XGBoost models against "treat all" and "treat none" strategies across threshold probabilities of 1–99%. The shaded region (20–60%) denotes the clinically relevant threshold range.

**Figure 5.** Comparison of global SHAP importance (mean |SHAP|) and LightGBM split-based importance for the top 15 features, demonstrating concordance between the two methods.

strategies.outcome_handling: Records with missing Outcome1 were excluded from the analytical cohort (2450 -> 2413 after dropping 37 rows with missing Outcome1).

cleaning_steps_full.name: Outcome integrity & cohort definition

cleaning_steps_full.name: Primary vs Salvage mTESE classification

cleaning_steps_full.name: Free-text pathology parsing (NLP)
cleaning_steps_full.details: Custom regex/NLP pipeline parsed free-text bilateral pathology reports (Pathology-RT and Pathology-LT) into 22 quantitative features (per-side percentages, binary indicators, missingness flags) covering SCO, CSTH, Maturation Arrest stages 1-4, and Hypospermatogenesis. Percentage handling: regex extracts explicit % when present, else assigns 100 if the pattern is found. MA hierarchy enforced: if arrested at an earlier phase, later phases set to 0 (Spermatogonial → Spermatocytic → ImmatureRound → Elongated).

feature_selection_pipeline_paper1: Stage 1: Mann-Whitney U test (p < 0.05) for univariate filter on continuous features
Stage 2: Mutual information ranking against Outcome1
Stage 3: Recursive Feature Elimination with Random Forest (RFE-RF)
Final feature set = UNION across the three methods (51 features in Paper1; 36 features in PaperB after further pruning)

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Phase 1: Data Understanding and Cleaning for NOA ML Project
"""

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import re
import os
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.max_rows', 100)
pd.set_option('display.width', None)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', 100)

# Create necessary directories
os.makedirs('/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/1_Data/Processed', exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs('/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/2_Analysis/Phase1', exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs('/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/4_Figures/PNG', exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs('/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/4_Figures/TIFF', exist_ok=True)

print("="*80)
print("PHASE 1: DATA UNDERSTANDING AND CLEANING - NOA ML PROJECT")
print("="*80)

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv('/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/1_Data/Raw/original_dataset.csv')
print(f"\n📊 Dataset loaded: {df.shape[0]} rows × {df.shape[1]} columns")

# ============================================================================
# TASK 1: Examine Pathology columns
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "="*80)
print("EXAMINING PATHOLOGY COLUMNS")
print("="*80)

print("\n--- Sample Pathology-RT values ---")
pathology_rt_samples = df['Pathology-RT'].dropna().head(20)
for i, val in enumerate(pathology_rt_samples):
    print(f"{i+1}. {val}")

print("\n--- Sample Pathology-LT values ---")
pathology_lt_samples = df['Pathology-LT'].dropna().head(20)
for i, val in enumerate(pathology_lt_samples):
    print(f"{i+1}. {val}")

# ============================================================================
# TASK 2: Create Pathology Extraction Functions
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "="*80)
print("EXTRACTING PATHOLOGY VARIABLES")
print("="*80)

def extract_percentage(text, pattern):
    """Extract percentage value if given, otherwise return 100 if pattern found, 0 if not."""
    if pd.isna(text) or not text:
        return 0
    
    text = str(text).lower()
    
    # Try to find pattern with percentage
    # Handle patterns like "SCO (30%)" or "SCO 30%" or "SCO: 30%"
    percentage_patterns = [
        rf'{pattern}\s*[\(\[\:]?\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%',  # SCO (30%) or SCO 30%
        rf'{pattern}\s*=\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%',  # SCO = 30%
        rf'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%\s*{pattern}',  # 30% SCO
    ]
    
    for patt in percentage_patterns:
        match = re.search(patt, text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            return float(match.group(1))
    
    # Check if pattern exists without percentage
    if re.search(pattern, text, re.IGNORECASE):
        return 100
    
    return 0

def extract_sco(text):
    """Extract SCO (Sertoli Cell Only) with various spellings."""
    if pd.isna(text) or not text:
        return 0
    
    text = str(text).lower()
    
    # SCO patterns: "SCO", "s co", "sc o", "sertoli cell only", "sertolly cell only"
    sco_patterns = [
        r's\s*c\s*o',  # SCO with spaces
        r'sertoli?\s*cell\s*only',  # Sertoli cell only
        r'sertoll?y?\s*cell\s*only',  # Sertolly cell only
    ]
    
    # Try to find with percentage
    for patt in sco_patterns:
        percentage_patterns = [
            rf'{patt}\s*[\(\[\:]?\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%',
            rf'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%\s*{patt}',
            rf'{patt}\s*[\(\[]?\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*[\)\]]?',  # SCO (30) without %
        ]
        for pp in percentage_patterns:
            match = re.search(pp, text, re.IGNORECASE)
            if match:
                return float(match.group(1))
    
    # Check if any SCO pattern exists
    for patt in sco_patterns:
        if re.search(patt, text, re.IGNORECASE):
            return 100
    
    return 0

def extract_csth(text):
    """Extract CSTH (Complete Seminiferous Tubular Hyalinization)."""
    if pd.isna(text) or not text:
        return 0
    
    text = str(text).lower()
    
    # CSTH patterns
    csth_patterns = [
        r'c\s*s\s*t\s*h',  # CSTH with spaces
        r'complete\s*seminiferous\s*tubular\s*hyalinization',
        r'tubular\s*hyalinization',
    ]
    
    for patt in csth_patterns:
        percentage_patterns = [
            rf'{patt}\s*[\(\[\:]?\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%',
            rf'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%\s*{patt}',
            rf'{patt}\s*[\(\[]?\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*[\)\]]?',
        ]
        for pp in percentage_patterns:
            match = re.search(pp, text, re.IGNORECASE)
            if match:
                return float(match.group(1))
    
    for patt in csth_patterns:
        if re.search(patt, text, re.IGNORECASE):
            return 100
    
    return 0

def extract_maturation_arrest(text):
    """
    Extract Maturation Arrest phases with hierarchical logic.
    Returns: (MA_Spermatogonial, MA_Spermatocytic, MA_ImmatureRound, MA_Elongated)
    
    Hierarchical logic:
    - If arrested at Spermatogonial (earliest) → phases 2-4 = 0
    - If arrested at Spermatocytic → phases 3-4 = 0
    - If arrested at ImmatureRound → phase 4 = 0
    - If arrested at Elongated (latest) → all phases present
    """
    if pd.isna(text) or not text:
        return 0, 0, 0, 0
    
    text = str(text).lower()
    
    # Patterns for each phase
    spermatogonial_patterns = [r'spermatogoni', r'ma\s*at\s*spermatogoni']
    spermatocytic_patterns = [r'spermatocyt', r'ma\s*at\s*spermatocyt', r'primary\s*spermatocyt']
    immature_round_patterns = [r'immature\s*round', r'round\s*spermatid', r'ma\s*at\s*round']
    elongated_patterns = [r'elongat', r'ma\s*at\s*elongat']
    
    # General MA pattern
    ma_general = r'maturation\s*arrest|m\.?\s*a\.?'
    
    # Check for specific arrests
    def find_arrest_value(patterns, text):
        for patt in patterns:
            # Try with percentage
            for pp in [rf'{patt}\s*[\(\[\:]?\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%',
                      rf'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%\s*{patt}']:
                match = re.search(pp, text, re.IGNORECASE)
                if match:
                    return float(match.group(1))
            # Check existence
            if re.search(patt, text, re.IGNORECASE):
                return 100
        return 0
    
    spermatogonial = find_arrest_value(spermatogonial_patterns, text)
    spermatocytic = find_arrest_value(spermatocytic_patterns, text)
    immature_round = find_arrest_value(immature_round_patterns, text)
    elongated = find_arrest_value(elongated_patterns, text)
    
    # Apply hierarchical logic
    # If arrested at spermatogonial level, later phases cannot exist
    if spermatogonial > 0:
        spermatocytic = 0
        immature_round = 0
        elongated = 0
    elif spermatocytic > 0:
        immature_round = 0
        elongated = 0
    elif immature_round > 0:
        elongated = 0
    
    return spermatogonial, spermatocytic, immature_round, elongated

def extract_hypospermatogenesis(text):
    """Extract Hypospermatogenesis and Severe Hypospermatogenesis."""
    if pd.isna(text) or not text:
        return 0, 0
    
    text = str(text).lower()
    
    # Severe hypospermatogenesis patterns
    severe_patterns = [
        r'severe\s*hypospermatogenesis',
        r'marked\s*hypospermatogenesis',
    ]
    
    # Regular hypospermatogenesis
    hypo_patterns = [
        r'hypospermatogenesis',
        r'hypo\s*spermatogenesis',
    ]
    
    def find_value(patterns, text):
        for patt in patterns:
            for pp in [rf'{patt}\s*[\(\[\:]?\s*(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%',
                      rf'(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\s*%\s*{patt}']:
                match = re.search(pp, text, re.IGNORECASE)
                if match:
                    return float(match.group(1))
            if re.search(patt, text, re.IGNORECASE):
                return 100
        return 0
    
    severe = find_value(severe_patterns, text)
    hypo = find_value(hypo_patterns, text)
    
    # If severe is found, don't double count in regular
    if severe > 0:
        # Check if hypo was found separately (not as part of severe)
        text_without_severe = re.sub(r'severe\s*hypospermatogenesis|marked\s*hypospermatogenesis', '', text, flags=re.IGNORECASE)
        hypo = find_value(hypo_patterns, text_without_severe)
    
    return hypo, severe

# Apply extraction to create new columns
print("\nExtracting pathology features from RT column...")
df['SCO_RT'] = df['Pathology-RT'].apply(extract_sco)
df['CSTH_RT'] = df['Pathology-RT'].apply(extract_csth)

ma_rt = df['Pathology-RT'].apply(extract_maturation_arrest)
df['MA_Spermatogonial_RT'] = ma_rt.apply(lambda x: x[0])
df['MA_Spermatocytic_RT'] = ma_rt.apply(lambda x: x[1])
df['MA_ImmatureRound_RT'] = ma_rt.apply(lambda x: x[2])
df['MA_Elongated_RT'] = ma_rt.apply(lambda x: x[3])

hypo_rt = df['Pathology-RT'].apply(extract_hypospermatogenesis)
df['Hypospermatogenesis_RT'] = hypo_rt.apply(lambda x: x[0])
df['Severe_Hypospermatogenesis_RT'] = hypo_rt.apply(lambda x: x[1])

print("Extracting pathology features from LT column...")
df['SCO_LT'] = df['Pathology-LT'].apply(extract_sco)
df['CSTH_LT'] = df['Pathology-LT'].apply(extract_csth)

ma_lt = df['Pathology-LT'].apply(extract_maturation_arrest)
df['MA_Spermatogonial_LT'] = ma_lt.apply(lambda x: x[0])
df['MA_Spermatocytic_LT'] = ma_lt.apply(lambda x: x[1])
df['MA_ImmatureRound_LT'] = ma_lt.apply(lambda x: x[2])
df['MA_Elongated_LT'] = ma_lt.apply(lambda x: x[3])

hypo_lt = df['Pathology-LT'].apply(extract_hypospermatogenesis)
df['Hypospermatogenesis_LT'] = hypo_lt.apply(lambda x: x[0])
df['Severe_Hypospermatogenesis_LT'] = hypo_lt.apply(lambda x: x[1])

# ============================================================================
# TASK 3: Verify Extraction Results
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "="*80)
print("EXTRACTION VERIFICATION")
print("="*80)

pathology_cols = ['SCO_RT', 'CSTH_RT', 'MA_Spermatogonial_RT', 'MA_Spermatocytic_RT', 
                  'MA_ImmatureRound_RT', 'MA_Elongated_RT', 'Hypospermatogenesis_RT', 
                  'Severe_Hypospermatogenesis_RT',
                  'SCO_LT', 'CSTH_LT', 'MA_Spermatogonial_LT', 'MA_Spermatocytic_LT',
                  'MA_ImmatureRound_LT', 'MA_Elongated_LT', 'Hypospermatogenesis_LT',
                  'Severe_Hypospermatogenesis_LT']

print("\n--- Descriptive Statistics for Extracted Pathology Features ---")
print(df[pathology_cols].describe())

print("\n--- Value Counts for Key Features (non-zero values) ---")
for col in pathology_cols:
    non_zero = (df[col] > 0).sum()
    print(f"{col}: {non_zero} non-zero values ({non_zero/len(df)*100:.1f}%)")

# ============================================================================
# TASK 4: Investigate Primary vs Salvage mTESE
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "="*80)
print("INVESTIGATING PRIMARY VS SALVAGE mTESE")
print("="*80)

# Check Successful Sperm Retrieval columns
ssr_cols = [col for col in df.columns if 'Successful Sperm Retrieval' in col]
print(f"\nSuccessful Sperm Retrieval columns: {ssr_cols}")

for col in ssr_cols:
    print(f"\n--- {col} ---")
    print(f"Non-null count: {df[col].notna().sum()}")
    print(f"Sample values:\n{df[col].dropna().head(10).tolist()}")

# Check Outcome columns
outcome_cols = ['Outcome1', 'Outcome2', 'Outcome3', 'Outcome4']
print("\n--- Outcome Columns Analysis ---")
for col in outcome_cols:
    if col in df.columns:
        print(f"\n{col}:")
        print(f"  Non-null: {df[col].notna().sum()}")
        print(f"  Value counts:\n{df[col].value_counts()}")

# Determine Primary vs Salvage
# Primary: Only has Outcome1 (first surgery)
# Salvage: Has Outcome2, Outcome3, or Outcome4 (repeat surgeries)
df['is_Primary'] = df['Outcome2'].isna() & df['Outcome3'].isna() & df['Outcome4'].isna()
df['Salvage_Count'] = df[['Outcome2', 'Outcome3', 'Outcome4']].notna().sum(axis=1)

print(f"\n--- Primary vs Salvage Classification ---")
print(f"Primary mTESE (no follow-up surgeries): {df['is_Primary'].sum()} ({df['is_Primary'].sum()/len(df)*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Salvage mTESE (has follow-up): {(~df['is_Primary']).sum()} ({(~df['is_Primary']).sum()/len(df)*100:.1f}%)")

# ============================================================================
# TASK 5: Data Quality Analysis
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "="*80)
print("DATA QUALITY ANALYSIS")
print("="*80)

# Missing values analysis
missing_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Column': df.columns,
    'Missing_Count': df.isnull().sum().values,
    'Missing_Percent': (df.isnull().sum() / len(df) * 100).values,
})
missing_df = missing_df[missing_df['Missing_Count'] > 0].sort_values('Missing_Percent', ascending=False)

print("\n--- Columns with Missing Values (sorted by % missing) ---")
print(missing_df.to_string(index=False))

# Identify 100% missing columns
full_missing = missing_df[missing_df['Missing_Percent'] == 100]['Column'].tolist()
print(f"\n--- Columns with 100% Missing Values ---")
print(full_missing if full_missing else "None")

# Critical missing (>50%)
critical_missing = missing_df[missing_df['Missing_Percent'] > 50]['Column'].tolist()
print(f"\n--- Columns with >50% Missing Values ---")
print(critical_missing if critical_missing else "None")

# ============================================================================
# TASK 6: Outcome1 Analysis by Pathology Patterns
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "="*80)
print("OUTCOME1 ANALYSIS BY PATHOLOGY PATTERNS")
print("="*80)

# Filter for valid Outcome1
df_valid = df[df['Outcome1'].notna()].copy()
print(f"\nRecords with valid Outcome1: {len(df_valid)}")

# Success rate by pathology type
print("\n--- Outcome1 Success Rate by Pathology Pattern ---")

def calculate_success_rate(df, col, threshold=0):
    """Calculate success rate for patients with specific pathology."""
    positive = df[df[col] > threshold]
    if len(positive) > 0:
        success_rate = positive['Outcome1'].mean() * 100
        return len(positive), success_rate
    return 0, np.nan

for col in pathology_cols[:8]:  # RT columns
    count, rate = calculate_success_rate(df_valid, col)
    if count > 0:
        print(f"{col}: {count} patients, {rate:.1f}% success rate")

print("\n--- For LT pathology ---")
for col in pathology_cols[8:]:  # LT columns
    count, rate = calculate_success_rate(df_valid, col)
    if count > 0:
        print(f"{col}: {count} patients, {rate:.1f}% success rate")

# Combined patterns
print("\n--- Success Rate by Combined Patterns ---")

# Pure SCO (SCO > 50% on both sides)
pure_sco = df_valid[(df_valid['SCO_RT'] >= 50) | (df_valid['SCO_LT'] >= 50)]
print(f"Pure SCO (≥50%): {len(pure_sco)} patients, {pure_sco['Outcome1'].mean()*100:.1f}% success")

# CSTH present
csth_present = df_valid[(df_valid['CSTH_RT'] > 0) | (df_valid['CSTH_LT'] > 0)]
print(f"CSTH present: {len(csth_present)} patients, {csth_present['Outcome1'].mean()*100:.1f}% success")

# MA present
ma_cols_rt = ['MA_Spermatogonial_RT', 'MA_Spermatocytic_RT', 'MA_ImmatureRound_RT', 'MA_Elongated_RT']
ma_cols_lt = ['MA_Spermatogonial_LT', 'MA_Spermatocytic_LT', 'MA_ImmatureRound_LT', 'MA_Elongated_LT']
ma_present = df_valid[(df_valid[ma_cols_rt + ma_cols_lt] > 0).any(axis=1)]
print(f"MA present: {len(ma_present)} patients, {ma_present['Outcome1'].mean()*100:.1f}% success" if len(ma_present) > 0 else "MA present: 0 patients")

# Hypospermatogenesis present
hypo_present = df_valid[(df_valid['Hypospermatogenesis_RT'] > 0) | (df_valid['Hypospermatogenesis_LT'] > 0)]
print(f"Hypospermatogenesis: {len(hypo_present)} patients, {hypo_present['Outcome1'].mean()*100:.1f}% success" if len(hypo_present) > 0 else "Hypospermatogenesis: 0 patients")

# ============================================================================
# TASK 7: Primary vs Salvage Analysis
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "="*80)
print("PRIMARY vs SALVAGE mTESE ANALYSIS")
print("="*80)

primary = df_valid[df_valid['is_Primary']]
salvage = df_valid[~df_valid['is_Primary']]

print(f"\n--- Primary mTESE (First Surgery) ---")
print(f"Count: {len(primary)}")
print(f"Outcome1 Success Rate: {primary['Outcome1'].mean()*100:.1f}%")

print(f"\n--- Salvage mTESE (Repeat Surgery) ---")
print(f"Count: {len(salvage)}")
if len(salvage) > 0:
    print(f"Outcome1 Success Rate: {salvage['Outcome1'].mean()*100:.1f}%")
    print(f"Outcome2 Success Rate: {salvage['Outcome2'].mean()*100:.1f}%" if salvage['Outcome2'].notna().any() else "")

# ============================================================================
# TASK 8: Save Cleaned Dataset
# ============================================================================
print("\n" + "="*80)
print("SAVING CLEANED DATASET")
print("="*80)

# Define columns to keep
# Original columns + new pathology features + Primary/Salvage indicator
new_cols = pathology_cols + ['is_Primary', 'Salvage_Count']

# Save full dataset with new features
output_path = '/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/1_Data/Processed/cleaned_dataset_phase1.csv'
df.to_csv(output_path, index=False)
print(f"\n✓ Full cleaned dataset saved to: {output_path}")
print(f"  Shape: {df.shape}")

# Create summary of extracted features
summary_df = df[['ID'] + pathology_cols + ['Outcome1', 'is_Primary']].copy()
summary_path = '/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/1_Data/Processed/pathology_features_summary.csv'
summary_df.to_csv(summary_path, index=False)

print("\n✓ Phase 1 Data Processing Complete!")
```

```python
    if len(positive) > 0:
        rate = positive['Outcome1'].mean() * 100
        count = len(positive)
        success_rates.append({
            'Feature': col.replace('_RT', ''),
            'Side': 'RT',
            'Success_Rate': rate,
            'Count': count
        })

for col in lt_cols:
    positive = df_valid[df_valid[col] > 0]
    if len(positive) > 0:
        rate = positive['Outcome1'].mean() * 100
        count = len(positive)
        success_rates.append({
            'Feature': col.replace('_LT', ''),
            'Side': 'LT',
            'Success_Rate': rate,
            'Count': count
        })

sr_df = pd.DataFrame(success_rates)

fig2 = px.bar(sr_df, x='Feature', y='Success_Rate', color='Side',
              barmode='group', text='Count',
              title='Outcome1 Success Rate by Pathology Type',
              labels={'Success_Rate': 'Success Rate (%)', 'Feature': 'Pathology'},
              color_discrete_map={'RT': '#2E86AB', 'LT': '#F18F01'})
fig2.update_traces(textposition='outside')
fig2.update_layout(template='plotly_white', height=500, width=1000)
fig2.write_image('/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/4_Figures/PNG/success_rate_by_pathology.png', scale=2)
print("✓ Saved success_rate_by_pathology.png")

# ============================================================================
# Figure 3: Primary vs Salvage mTESE Outcome Comparison
# ============================================================================
primary_success = df_valid[df_valid['is_Primary']]['Outcome1'].mean() * 100
salvage_success = df_valid[~df_valid['is_Primary']]['Outcome1'].mean() * 100
primary_count = df_valid[df_valid['is_Primary']].shape[0]
salvage_count = df_valid[~df_valid['is_Primary']].shape[0]

fig3 = go.Figure()
fig3.add_trace(go.Bar(
    x=['Primary mTESE', 'Salvage mTESE'],
    y=[primary_success, salvage_success],
    text=[f'n={primary_count}', f'n={salvage_count}'],
    textposition='outside',
    marker_color=['#2E86AB', '#F18F01']
))
fig3.update_layout(
    title='Primary vs Salvage mTESE: Outcome1 Success Rate',
    yaxis_title='Success Rate (%)',
    template='plotly_white',
    height=400,
    width=600
)
fig3.write_image('/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/4_Figures/PNG/primary_vs_salvage.png', scale=2)
print("✓ Saved primary_vs_salvage.png")

# ============================================================================
# Figure 4: Missing Values Heatmap (Key Columns)
# ============================================================================
key_cols = ['Age', 'Partner Age', 'FSH', 'LH', 'Testosterone levels', 'Prolactin', 
            'Pathology-RT', 'Pathology-LT', 'Outcome1']
missing_pct = df[key_cols].isnull().mean() * 100

fig4 = go.Figure(go.Bar(
    x=missing_pct.values,
    y=key_cols,
    orientation='h',
    marker_color=['#2E86AB' if x < 50 else '#E63946' for x in missing_pct.values],
    text=[f'{x:.1f}%' for x in missing_pct.values],
    textposition='outside'
))
fig4.update_layout(
    title='Missing Values in Key Clinical Variables',
    xaxis_title='Missing %',
    yaxis_title='Variable',
    template='plotly_white',
    height=400,
    width=700
)
fig4.write_image('/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/4_Figures/PNG/missing_values_key_vars.png', scale=2)
print("✓ Saved missing_values_key_vars.png")

# ============================================================================
# Figure 5: Outcome Distribution Pie Chart
# ============================================================================
outcome_counts = df_valid['Outcome1'].value_counts()

fig5 = go.Figure(go.Pie(
    values=outcome_counts.values,
    labels=['Unsuccessful (0)', 'Successful (1)'],
    marker_colors=['#E63946', '#2E86AB'],
    hole=0.4
))
fig5.update_layout(
    title=f'Outcome1 Distribution (n={len(df_valid)})',
    template='plotly_white',
    height=400,
    width=500
)
fig5.write_image('/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/4_Figures/PNG/outcome_distribution.png', scale=2)
print("✓ Saved outcome_distribution.png")

# ============================================================================
# Figure 6: Combined Dashboard
# ============================================================================
fig6 = make_subplots(
    rows=2, cols=2,
    subplot_titles=['Pathology Feature Counts (RT)', 'Success Rate by Pathology (RT)',
                    'Outcome1 Distribution', 'Primary vs Salvage Success'],
    specs=[[{'type': 'bar'}, {'type': 'bar'}],
           [{'type': 'pie'}, {'type': 'bar'}]]
)

# Subplot 1: RT counts
fig6.add_trace(go.Bar(x=feature_names, y=rt_counts, marker_color='#2E86AB', name='RT Count'),
               row=1, col=1)

# Subplot 2: RT success rates
rt_sr = sr_df[sr_df['Side'] == 'RT']
fig6.add_trace(go.Bar(x=rt_sr['Feature'], y=rt_sr['Success_Rate'], marker_color='#F18F01', 
                      name='Success %'), row=1, col=2)

# Subplot 3: Outcome pie
fig6.add_trace(go.Pie(values=outcome_counts.values, 
                      labels=['Unsuccessful', 'Successful'],
                      marker_colors=['#E63946', '#2E86AB'],
                      domain=dict(x=[0, 0.45], y=[0, 0.45])),
               row=2, col=1)

# Subplot 4: Primary vs Salvage
fig6.add_trace(go.Bar(x=['Primary', 'Salvage'], y=[primary_success, salvage_success],
                      marker_color=['#2E86AB', '#F18F01'], name='Success Rate'),
               row=2, col=2)

fig6.update_layout(title='Phase 1 Summary Dashboard', height=800, width=1200, 
                   template='plotly_white', showlegend=False)
fig6.write_image('/home/ubuntu/NOA_ML_Project/4_Figures/PNG/phase1_summary_dashboard.png', scale=2)

print("\n✓ All visualizations saved!")
```

#### Statistical Summaries and Findings
- Dataset: **2450 rows, 55 original columns**
- After feature engineering: **73 columns**
- Valid `Outcome1`: **2413 records**
- `Outcome1` distribution: **1527 failure (63.3%)**, **886 success (36.7%)**
- Primary vs Salvage classification (all records):
  - Primary: **2234 (91.2%)**
  - Salvage: **216 (8.8%)**
- Primary vs Salvage with valid Outcome1:
  - Primary: **2203**, success **32.5%**
  - Salvage: **210**, success **80.5%**

#### Pathology Feature Extraction Summary
16 features generated (8 RT + 8 LT):
- SCO, CSTH
- MA_Spermatogonial, MA_Spermatocytic, MA_ImmatureRound, MA_Elongated
- Hypospermatogenesis, Severe_Hypospermatogenesis

Non-zero prevalence highlights:
- RT: SCO 50.6%, CSTH 28.0%, MA_Spermatocytic 23.1%
- LT: SCO 35.3%, CSTH 18.9%, MA_Spermatocytic 14.6%

Outcome association highlights:
- Highest success: Hypospermatogenesis (RT 91.1%, LT 81.0%)
- Lowest success: SCO (RT 32.4%, LT 26.9%)

Combined pattern outcomes:
- Pure SCO (≥50%): 32.3%
- CSTH present: 43.9%
- MA present: 41.0%
- Hypospermatogenesis present: 88.3%

#### Missing Data Findings
9 columns at 100% missing (exclude from modeling):
- Cancer Type
- Cancer Treatment
- Torsion of spermatic cord
- Surgery Therapeutic
- Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin
- TSH
- inhibin B
- AMH
- Infertility length

#### Key Insights and Recommendations
1. **Hypospermatogenesis** appears strongly positive for retrieval success.
2. **SCO** is associated with the weakest outcomes.
3. **Salvage cohort** shows very high success; evaluate potential selection bias before inference.
4. Exclude 100%-missing variables from modeling pipeline.
5. Use controlled imputation for moderate missingness variables (FSH, LH, Testosterone).
6. Consider stratified modeling by surgery cohort (Primary vs Salvage).

#### Next Steps (Phase 2 Readiness)
- Perform univariate + multivariate statistical tests for feature significance.
- Estimate adjusted effect sizes for pathology and endocrine predictors.
- Build candidate feature sets (baseline clinical vs pathology-enriched).
- Define robust validation design (stratified CV with cohort-aware splits).

literature_review.master_table_raw_markdown:
# Machine Learning for Predicting Sperm Retrieval Success in Non-Obstructive Azoospermia: A Comprehensive Literature Review and Project Analysis

**Report Generated:** 2026-04-27

## Executive summary

Non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) represents the most severe manifestation of male factor infertility, defined by the absence of sperm in the ejaculate due to failed spermatogenesis. For these patients, microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) offers the only possibility for biological fatherhood, yet its success is unpredictable, with sperm retrieval rates (SSR) varying between 30% and 60% [3, 4, 6]. The invasive nature, emotional toll, and significant cost of the procedure have created an urgent clinical need for accurate, non-invasive preoperative predictive tools. This report provides a comprehensive literature review of research from 2020 to 2026 on the application of machine learning (ML) to predict micro-TESE outcomes, synthesizes best practices from the field, and offers a comparative analysis with a novel, large-scale project initiative.

The analysis of recent literature reveals a rapid maturation in the use of computational models to augment clinical decision-making for NOA. These models leverage diverse patient data—including demographics, comprehensive hormonal profiles, clinical history, genetic markers, and histopathology—to generate individualized predictions of procedural success. The predictive performance of these models varies, with reported Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC) values ranging from a moderate 0.72 to an exceptional high of 0.95 [1, 3, 9, 13]. Across multiple studies, a core set of features consistently demonstrates predictive value, including serum levels of follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), luteinizing hormone (LH), testosterone, and inhibin B, along with testicular volume, patient age, and the presence of genetic anomalies such as Klinefelter syndrome [3, 4, 5, 6, 9].

Recent innovations have introduced powerful new predictors. A 2026 study highlighted rete testis thickness (RTT), an ultrasound-derived biomarker, achieving an AUC of 0.950 when combined with hormonal data, suggesting a new frontier in imaging-based prediction [1]. Other models have refined the use of hormonal data, identifying the testosterone-to-estradiol (T/E2) ratio and testosterone-to-luteinizing hormone (T/LH) ratio as significant, albeit complex, variables [3, 13]. Histopathological data remains a cornerstone, with findings like hypospermatogenesis being strongly positive predictors, while Sertoli cell-only (SCO) syndrome is a negative indicator.

The literature review also established a clear consensus on methodological best practices essential for developing robust and generalizable models. These include rigorous validation strategies beyond simple data splits, such as external or temporal validation, and nested cross-validation to prevent optimistic performance bias [6, 7]. The importance of handling missing data and class imbalance appropriately, preferably within cross-validation loops, is a recurrent theme. Furthermore, the adoption of explainable AI (XAI) techniques, such as SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), is critical for demystifying "black box" models, fostering clinical trust, and uncovering the drivers of individual predictions [15]. Other responsible AI practices, including calibration analysis and Decision Curve Analysis (DCA), are highlighted as necessary steps to evaluate the real-world clinical utility and safety of these models.

A comparative analysis was performed against a new project initiative detailed in the provided documentation. This project stands out due to its planned scope and several key elements of novelty. Its dataset, comprising 2,450 patients, is among the largest reported for this specific task, surpassing most cohorts in the reviewed literature, with the exception of one multi-center study that was not ML-focused [5]. The project’s most significant innovation lies in its feature engineering: the automated extraction of 16 granular, quantitative histopathology indicators from free-text biopsy reports using a custom NLP parser. This includes a unique hierarchical encoding of maturation arrest stages, a level of detail not observed in other studies. While the project documentation outlines ambitious plans for comparing 15 ML models and conducting 11 distinct validation tests, these phases are not yet complete. If executed as planned, this comprehensive evaluation would represent a new benchmark for methodological rigor in the field.

Based on this analysis, concrete recommendations for the project include leveraging the established best practices for validation (nested cross-validation, temporal split), employing methods like SMOTE for class imbalance, and integrating XAI, calibration, and DCA into the modeling pipeline. A recommended final feature set for a clinical decision support system (CDSS) would incorporate the project's novel pathology features alongside the literature-validated predictors: FSH, total testosterone, inhibin B, testicular volume, and patient age. While the current body of literature is highly promising, it is largely characterized by retrospective, single-center studies. There remains a critical need for large, multi-center prospective validation to confirm the generalizability of these AI models before they can be responsibly integrated into routine clinical practice as trusted decision-support tools.

## Search scope and inclusion criteria

This report synthesizes and analyzes a curated collection of research literature focused on the application of machine learning (ML) and artificial intelligence (AI) to the field of male infertility, with a specific emphasis on predicting surgical sperm retrieval (SSR) success in patients with non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) undergoing microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE). The analysis is based exclusively on a set of provided research files and project documentation; no new external web searches were conducted. The information integrity of this report relies solely on the content within these supplied sources.

The primary inclusion criterion for this review was direct relevance to the development, validation, or application of ML models for predicting SSR in NOA. The time frame for this primary literature spans from 2020 to 2026, capturing the most recent advancements in the field. Core papers meeting this criterion include studies that detail specific model architectures (e.g., Random Forest, Gradient-Boosted Trees, Artificial Neural Networks), report performance metrics (e.g., Area Under the Curve, sensitivity, specificity), and identify key predictive features from patient data. The primary papers central to this review are **Li et al. (2026)** [1], **Kobayashi et al. (2023)** [13], **Bachelot et al. (2023)** [9], **Zeadna et al. (2020)** [6], **Liu et al. (2023)** [4], **Deng et al. (2023)** [3], and **Ceyhan et al. (2025)** [5].

A secondary inclusion criterion encompassed papers from the broader domain of andrology and male infertility that, while not exclusively focused on NOA prediction, provide critical context and establish methodological best practices applicable to the primary research question. These papers address topics such as AI-driven semen analysis, prediction of varicocelectomy outcomes, forecasting of assisted reproductive technology (ART) success, and implementation of responsible AI. Key papers in this category include **Ory et al. (2022)** [11] on varicocele repair prediction, **Goss et al. (2026)** [10] on real-time sperm identification, and several reviews and tutorials, such as those by **Ibañez Vazquez et al. (2026)** [8], **Verratti et al. (2024)** [2], and **Lee and Yafi (2023)** [7], which provide comprehensive guidance on validation techniques, explainability, and clinical deployment.

The synthesis aims to create a cohesive and comprehensive narrative, drawing from both primary and secondary sources to build a deep understanding of the current state of the art, identify established best practices, and situate a new research project within the existing body of knowledge. All substantive claims and recommendations made in this report are directly supported by citations from this curated set of documents.

## Master table of all included papers

The following table synthesizes key information from all primary and secondary research papers included in this literature review. Papers are categorized by their primary relevance: "NOA/mTESE Prediction" for studies directly modeling sperm retrieval success, and "Best Practices / Related Fields" for those providing methodological guidance or covering adjacent topics in andrology.
